title: How Some Fish Can Recognize Themselves in Mirrors
summary: Cleaner wrasse have challenged assumptions about fish cognition by using mirrors to inspect body marks, suggesting sophisticated visual self-referencing without proving human-like self-awareness.

lesson.learn: Mirror self-recognition was once treated as a club for a few large-brained animals, but cleaner wrasse have complicated that story. In experiments, fish first react to a mirror as if it shows another fish, then gradually change behavior. Some inspect themselves, adjust posture, and respond to marks placed on parts of the body they can see only in reflection. That does not mean the fish has human-like self-awareness or inner language. It does suggest the animal can connect the mirror image to its own body well enough to guide action. Cleaner wrasse may be especially suited to this because their social life depends on close visual inspection of other fish.
lesson.simpler: Cleaner wrasse can use mirrors to inspect marks on their bodies, showing advanced body awareness even if it is not the same as human self-awareness.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Cleaner wrasse mirror behavior shows that some fish can use reflected visual information as self-related body information, challenging simple assumptions about animal intelligence.
lesson.deeperDive: The mirror test was originally designed around animals that groom or touch visible marks, such as apes. Fish cannot touch themselves with hands, so researchers looked for species-appropriate behavior: scraping a marked area after seeing it in a mirror. Critics argue that passing the test may not equal rich self-consciousness, while supporters argue that dismissing fish performance exposes bias in how cognition is measured. Later studies have explored whether cleaner wrasse can recognize themselves in photographs or use mirrors for body-size judgments. The strongest interpretation is careful: some fish can integrate reflection, body map, and action in ways once thought unlikely.
lesson.funFact: Cleaner wrasse normally inspect other fish for parasites, which may help explain their mirror-test skill.
